content.title: Fundamentals of Visualization
content.text: Data visualization is the practice of turning raw, complex numbers into graphical elements like charts, graphs, and maps. Its primary goal is to reveal hidden insights and share them effectively across a business organization.
list: Definition: Mapping numerical values to visual attributes (length, color, position).
Goal: Transform 'math' into 'pictures' our brains can understand 60,000x faster.
Scope: From static reports to interactive real-time dashboards.

content.title: Psychology & Cognitive Benefits
content.text: Why do we visualize? Because human perception is hard-wired for visuals. Visualization cuts decision mistakes by 80% and boosts memory retention.
content.text_2: In a dashboard setting, using color-coded alerts (Green for high, Red for low) ensures the team 'remembers' where help is needed without scanning deep tables.

content.title: The Data Visualization Process
content.text: Creating effective visuals follows a modular pipeline. Each stage is critical: if research is poor (Acquire), the final dashboard will be misleading (Render).
list: Acquire & Filter: Gathering raw data and cleaning outliers.
Represent: Choosing the right chart (Bar vs Line).
Refine: Adding context, labels, and removing clutter.

content.title: Core Analysis Techniques
content.text: Before presenting data, an analyst must perform deep exploration to find the 'story' inside the numbers.
list: Univariate Analysis: Looking at one variable alone (e.g., Histogram of profits) to see its shape or spread.
Exploratory Analysis (EDA): Poking around data with simple charts to find surprises or outliers without a fixed plan.
Descriptive Analysis: Using Summary Stats (Box Plots) to find the 'middle' 50% of your data performance.

content.title: Global Chart Catalog
content.text: Choosing the right chart is the difference between a clear message and total confusion.

content.title: Design Principles & Perception
content.text: Good design follows 'Gestalt' rules so the eye automatically groups related information.
list: Proximity: Items close together are seen as a group (e.g., regional bars).
Similarity: Items with the same color are linked (e.g., 'Action' genre across years).
Position vs Color: Brains rank Position (x/y) as the most accurate feature, and Color as the least accurate for numbers.

content.title: Best Practices vs Common Mistakes
content.text: Efficiency in visualization comes from minimalism. Avoid 'chart-junk'—decorative elements that don't add value—and focus on the signal-to-noise ratio.
list: Do: Use clear labels, start axes at zero for bar charts, and use color sparingly.
Don't: Use 3D effects, over-clutter with multiple fonts, or use pie charts for more than 5 categories.
content.text_2: Common mistakes include using 3D effects (distorts size), over-labeling, and using rainbow colors that confuse the meaning.

content.title: Unified Case Study: Superstore Analytics
content.text: Applying everything to our sample Superstore dataset (10,000+ orders).
list: Exploration: A Histogram of profits shows most are $100-500, but a Scatter plot reveals 'Profit Leaks' where high sales have low profit due to huge discounts.
Process: We Acquire the CSV, Clean return data, Map Sales to a Line chart (showing a Q4 holiday boom), and Render a Power BI Dashboard.
Impact: By spotting that 'Furniture' has high sales but negative profit in the East, managers can cut discounts and boost tech focus, increasing total profit by 15%.

assignments.title: Interactive Superstore Dashboard
assignments.description: Using Power BI or Tableau, create a 3-page dashboard that answers: 1) Which region is most profitable? 2) Is there a link between Discount % and Profit? 3) Which month has the highest sales spike?

assignments.title: Visual Perception Critique
assignments.description: Find a 'bad' chart from a news source. Rewrite its story using Gestalt principles and provide an improved alternative using the correct chart type.
